metadata.summary: AI-augmented research ops for N >= 500 sessions/week; 9-stage pipeline with frozen codebook. Haiku for throughput, Sonnet for synthesis, Opus for mining with human gates before publication.

content.title: User Research at Scale
content.summary: AI-augmented research operations for N >= 500 sessions/week or >= 50 unmoderated tests, running a 9-stage pipeline: intake → sampling → instrumentation → collection → transcription → coding → synthesis → review → publish. Uses a frozen codebook with a separate proposed_codes overflow channel to prevent theme drift, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints before synthesis is published.

why.p: Traditional manual research does not scale with product velocity. AI handles throughput (transcription, pattern detection, codebook tagging); humans handle strategy, interpretation, and empathy. The separation is enforced by model assignment: Haiku for mechanical tasks, Sonnet for structured synthesis, Opus for cross-segment pattern mining. Mixing them collapses the quality/cost trade-off.

list.item: N >= 500 sessions/week or >= 50 unmoderated tests where manual coding is the bottleneck.
Continuous discovery teams needing a weekly pulse (Teresa Torres cadence).
Product orgs with multiple teams running parallel studies (research-as-platform).
Localization at scale — same study across 5+ languages, AI handles transcription + translation.
Survey + behavior + interview triangulation when a single researcher cannot read everything.
Small N (less than 10 deep interviews) — AI noise overwhelms signal; human coding is faster and richer.
Strategic generative discovery where pattern-recognition beats throughput.
Sensitive/regulated topics (health, finance, minors) requiring manual consent chains.
Early-stage startups with less than 100 users — you do not have scale problems yet.
Studies where rapport, body language, or longitudinal trust is the data.
1. intake: convert PM brief to study spec, screener, hypotheses
2. sampling: build quotas, randomize, dedupe panel respondents
3. instrumentation: configure unmoderated test scripts in Maze/Userlytics
4. collection: execute tests and gather session recordings
5. transcription: forced-aligned transcription with diarization, PII redaction
6. coding: apply frozen codebook, tag quotes with codes and valence
7. synthesis: cross-segment pattern mining, confidence-tiered findings
8. review: human-in-the-loop checkpoint before publication
9. publish: push themes/sentiment to Looker/Notion/Dovetail

rule.statement: Run theme-coder and bias-auditor in parallel; gate report-writer on a researcher human-in-loop checkpoint at step 8.
rule.rationale: AI synthesis can proceed while bias audit runs — they are independent. But publishing without a human checkpoint after synthesis lets AI-invented "insights" reach stakeholders without review.

rule.statement: Assign Haiku to mechanical tasks (panel-sampler, protocol-runner, transcript-cleaner, dashboard-publisher); Sonnet to structured synthesis (theme-coder, quote-curator, bias-auditor); Opus to cross-segment pattern mining and executive synthesis.
rule.rationale: This assignment minimizes cost while matching model capability to task complexity. Inverting it — Opus on transcript cleaning, Haiku on pattern mining — either burns tokens or degrades quality.

list.item: research-intake (Sonnet): PM brief → study-spec.md + screener JSON
panel-sampler (Haiku): quotas, randomize, dedupe → participant CSV
protocol-runner (Haiku): execute unmoderated tests in Maze/Userlytics → session IDs
transcript-cleaner (Haiku): strip filler, redact PII, normalize timestamps → clean VTT/JSON
theme-coder (Sonnet): apply codebook, tag quotes with codes + valence → coded JSONL
pattern-analyst (Opus): cross-segment clustering, confidence-tiered findings → findings draft
quote-curator (Sonnet): representative + outlier quotes per finding → quote bank
report-writer (Opus): executive synthesis, recommendations, confidence tiers → final report MD
bias-auditor (Sonnet): leading phrasing, sample bias detection → audit notes
dashboard-publisher (Haiku): push themes/sentiment to Looker/Notion/Dovetail → URLs

rule.statement: Lock the codebook before collection; version it (v1.0.0) and bump only with documented diffs; use a separate proposed_codes overflow channel instead of modifying the codebook mid-study.
rule.rationale: Theme drift across batches is the #1 AI coding failure mode. A frozen codebook with a version + proposed_codes channel surfaces emergent themes for human review without corrupting the existing taxonomy.

example.note: Theme-coder prompt with frozen codebook and proposed_codes channel:
example.code: <task>code-transcript</task> <codebook src=".aidocs/research/codebook.yaml"/> <rules> <rule>One quote may receive multiple codes; record span offsets.</rule> <rule>Reject codes not in codebook. Emit proposed_codes list instead.</rule> <rule>Tag valence in {-2,-1,0,1,2} with one-line evidence.</rule> <rule>If transcript is less than 80% English confidence, halt and request translation.</rule> </rules> <output schema="coded_quote_v1">JSONL</output> <gotcha>Do not summarize. Preserve verbatim spans.</gotcha>

rule.statement: Sample 10% of AI-coded segments for human re-coding every batch; track inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa >= 0.7) and recalibrate when it drops below threshold.
rule.rationale: AI coding accuracy drifts as transcripts vary in quality and subject matter. Kappa tracking catches drift before it contaminates an entire study's findings.

list.item: whisperx: forced-aligned transcription with diarization, faster than vanilla Whisper
pyannote-audio: speaker diarization when WhisperX is insufficient
ffmpeg: slice session recordings into per-task clips for AI scoring
yt-dlp: pull session videos from CDN URLs given by panel platforms
jq / duckdb: query coded JSONL transcripts and platform exports
pandas + sentence-transformers: embed and cluster open-ended survey responses
bertopic: topic modeling on large response corpora
dvc / lakefs: version control for transcripts + codebooks (audit trail)
presidio: PII redaction before sending to external LLMs

rule.statement: Gate on Whisper confidence score >= 0.6 per transcript before coding — low-quality audio produces confident-but-wrong text that the coder cannot distinguish from accurate transcription.
rule.rationale: Downstream coding errors from bad transcription are undetectable at the finding layer. The confidence gate catches the problem at the source.

list.item: UserTesting (enterprise): REST API + webhook; largest panel, AI Insights add-on
Userlytics (mid+): REST API; 2M+ panel, AI analytics, EU-friendly
Maze (self-serve+): API + Zapier; bias detection, dynamic follow-ups
dscout (enterprise): API; mobile ethnography and longitudinal diaries
Dovetail (all tiers): REST + GraphQL; repository + AI tagging, codebook host
Sprig (mid): API + JS SDK; in-product micro-surveys at session scale
Hotjar / FullStory (mid): API; session replay + heatmaps for behavior layer
Rally / User Interviews (mid): API; recruitment and scheduling automation

rule.statement: Cache full transcripts in the LLM context using prompt caching when coding hundreds of quotes against the same codebook — the savings are substantial at scale.
rule.rationale: Repeated codebook context is identical across all transcript coding calls. Prompt caching eliminates the redundant token cost of loading the codebook on every call.

rule.statement: Publish disconfirming quotes alongside confirming ones — outliers are signal, not noise.
rule.rationale: LLM summarization compresses outliers. The most valuable insights often come from the user who had the unexpected reaction. Requiring outlier inclusion in the quote bank prevents systematic suppression.

rule.statement: Stratify panels by behavior (active/lapsed/new), not just demographics — demographic-only sampling hides usage truth.
rule.rationale: Demographic similarity does not predict usage similarity. Active and churned users of the same age have opposite perspectives on the product's value.

rule.statement: Triangulate every finding with AI sentiment + behavioral analytics + verbatim quote — never report from one source alone.
rule.rationale: Single-source findings have low reliability at scale. Three-source triangulation with confidence tiers (high >= 3 types, medium = 2, low = 1) enables stakeholders to calibrate trust.

rule.statement: Add a "draft/published" gate on every finding — dashboards auto-update faster than humans can vet.
rule.rationale: Unvetted AI findings reaching a product meeting without a human review can redirect roadmap decisions based on hallucinated patterns.

antipattern.description: Running sentiment analysis on non-English transcripts without routing by language.
antipattern.reason: Sentiment models miss sarcasm, code-switching, and culture-specific affect in non-English text. Polarity flips on negation in low-resource languages without language-aware routing.

antipattern.description: Letting the theme-coder invent codes when the codebook is unclear rather than using the proposed_codes overflow channel.
antipattern.reason: Invented codes contaminate the taxonomy and produce theme drift across batches. Strict allow-list enforcement with a separate proposed_codes channel keeps the codebook clean.

antipattern.description: Hallucinating participant IDs in the report when citing quotes.
antipattern.reason: Agents invent participant IDs that do not exist in the corpus. Require verbatim span offsets (start_char, end_char) tied to a specific session file, not paraphrases.

antipattern.description: Re-running coding on the same transcript without caching, burning 10x tokens.
antipattern.reason: Hash the transcript content and cache the coded output. Skip transcripts that already have a cached result. Uncached re-runs are pure waste.

antipattern.description: Feeding full transcript bundles > 200k tokens into a long-context model without chunking.
antipattern.reason: Long-context models silently truncate at token boundaries. Chunk at session boundaries with overlap and verify completeness before synthesis.

antipattern.description: Pattern-analyst over-indexing on the first segment in the prompt.
antipattern.reason: LLMs weight earlier context more heavily. Randomize segment order across runs and compare findings to detect position bias.

rule.statement: Redact PII with Presidio before any external LLM call; confirm that platform recordings have consented to AI processing before feeding into the pipeline.
rule.rationale: Sending raw PII to a non-zero-retention LLM endpoint violates GDPR and platform ToS. Presidio redaction runs locally before any external call. Consent flags must be read by the transcript-cleaner before quoting.

rule.statement: Never present synthetic users or AI-generated personas as real research findings.
rule.rationale: Synthetic personas leak training-data clichés and introduce systematic bias toward the modal user in the training set. They are planning tools, not research evidence.

antipattern.description: Mixing timestamp formats from different platforms (Maze milliseconds, UserTesting ISO, Otter seconds) without normalization.
antipattern.reason: Mixed timestamps break quote offset matching and prevent cross-platform de-duplication. Normalize in the transcript-cleaner stage before any downstream use.

antipattern.description: Agents quietly dropping non-English sessions when the prompt does not explicitly handle them.
antipattern.reason: Silently dropping sessions produces a biased corpus. Assert language coverage in tests and route non-English sessions to a translation step before coding.

section.name: where-it-fails
section.p: Sentiment models miss sarcasm, code-switching, and culture-specific affect (especially non-English). AI auto-coding produces theme drift across batches if no fixed taxonomy is locked.

Synthetic users / AI panels still bias toward training distribution; do not substitute for real users for novel UX. Bias detection (e.g., Maze) flags syntax not intent — leading questions slip through if phrased neutrally.
Recordings without screen-region annotation lead AI to hallucinate user actions. LLM summarization compresses outliers — the most valuable quotes are the ones that get smoothed away.
Cross-demographic clustering tends to reproduce platform demographics, not target market. No causal inference — patterns at scale still need experimental validation.

template.purpose: Frozen taxonomy: codes with id/label/valence_default/examples, segment axes
Batch-codes transcript directory via claude CLI, deduplicates, outputs parquet
